metadata.title: Mission Hill Project
metadata.author: Emily Sandercock
metadata.subtitle: Public and vocational education before the housing project

caption.text: This map, from 1931, is the final Bromley Atlas available before either the Mission Main Project or the Mission Hill Extension was built.
caption.title: Mission Hill in 1931

caption.text: Mission Main was constructed on these streets between [1938 and 1941](https://www.bostonhousing.org/en/Departments/Planning-and-Real-Estate-Development/Mixed-Finance-Development/Mission-Main.aspx), making it one of the earliest public housing projects in the city.
caption.title: The site of Mission Main

caption.text: In 1952, Mission Main was expanded here to become the Mission Extension. In contrast to Mission Main, buildings here were three stories, rather than larger apartment blocks.
caption.title: The site of Mission Extension

caption.text: Built in the early 1900s, the Ira Allen School borders the site of the Mission Hill Extension….
caption.title: Ira Allen

caption.text: One of the earliest photographs of the Ira Allen School was taken between 1920 and 1930. Visible in the background is the building labelled a Hellenic Church on the Bromley Atlas.
caption.title: Ira Allen School

caption.text: The Ira Allen School is now owned by Wentworth Institute of Technology. The same church from the early 20th century photograph is visible in this picture.
caption.title: Ira Allen today

caption.text: In 1906, the streets below the Ira Allen School were divided into individual lots…
But by 1915 the Boston Industrial School had been built on this plot of land.

This picture, taken as a project of the Work Projects Administration in 1938, shows a room in the Boston Industrial School, revealing that the school did not only contain classrooms, but also what seems to have been a library.
caption.title: The Boston Industrial School

caption.text: This book, published in 1877, offered guidance on vocational training within Boston, which would have been intended for schools like the Ira Allen School and the Boston Industrial School…
caption.title: Report on Schools and School-Shops

caption.text: The report was focused on questions of what responsibilities educators had to students, especially in a “radically changing world,” where apprenticeships were becoming far less common. Public, vocational training was envisioned as the modern version of the old apprentice system.
caption.title: Report detail

caption.text: The building that served as the Boston Industrial School still stands today, though it is now owned by the Wentworth Institute of Technology. The concrete façade above the front door contains a Latin motto, while a recent addition below that façade identifies the building as part of the Wentworth Institute of Technology.
caption.title: Boston Industrial today

caption.text: Wentworth was granted a charter by the Commonwealth of Massachusetts in 1904, it was founded to provide students with mechanical training, originally not requiring that students entering already have a high school diploma. [The first Black student graduated from Wentworth in 1913.](https://wit.edu/about/inclusive-excellence/wentworths-diversity-past-present)
caption.title: Wentworth Institute of Technology

caption.text: Around the same years, Black students at Harvard were protesting discrimination at the university, [particularly the segregation of dorms.](https://legacyofslaveryreport.harvard.edu/report/the-long-legacies-of-slavery-segregation-marginalization-and-resistance-at-harvard#abbott-lawrence-lowell-and-discrimination-in-admissions-and-housing) Edwin Jourdain Jr, pictured here, would lead the fight over the segregation of the dorms, before beginning a career opposing gerrymandering in Chicago.
caption.title: Edwin Jourdain Jr.

caption.text: Meanwhile, women, who were excluded from Wentworth until the 1970s, were organizing for voting rights within colleges where they were admitted, such as Radcliffe, and in partnership with trade unions such as the [Women's Trade Union League.](https://www-jstor-org.ezproxy.neu.edu/stable/1903256?seq=8&site=jstor)
caption.title: College Equal Suffrage League

caption.text: This photo shows Wentworth Institute in 1920. The picture is from the front of the building, and so the street that would contain the Mission Extension is to the left side of the building.
caption.title: Wentworth Institute

caption.text: In 1948, four years before the Mission Extension opened, Wentworth welcomed 750 students at the beginning of the school year.
caption.title: Class of 1948

caption.text: The institute did not continuously expand, however, in 1953, a year after the Mission Extension opened, 600 students started at Wentworth in the fall.
caption.title: Class of 1953

caption.text: This photograph from 2026 shows Mission Extension today, now known by a different name, but still owned by the Boston Housing Authority. Students from the surrounding universities mingle with residents of the public housing complex.
caption.title: Mission Extension today

caption.text: Northeastern University, associated with the YMCA according to the 1931 Bromley Atlas, is another university, like Wentworth, that still borders the public housing here.
caption.title: Northeastern University

caption.text: Like Wentworth, Northeastern was opened with the goal of preparing students for employment, and this was reflected in programs organized by the university. During co-op, described here, students joined businesses for short periods of training while enrolled at the university.
caption.title: Northeastern Co-Op

caption.text: The Northeastern mascot is a husky. Once, there was a real dog that served as the mascot of the university, though now the role is taken by a person in a costume.
caption.title: The Northeastern husky

caption.text: This neighborhood would become the site for Mission Main, which predated the Extension, with ground broken on construction in the late 1930s...
caption.title: Mission Main

caption.text: This neighborhood also hosted educational institutions, though here they were also religiously focused.
caption.title: Mission Church School

caption.text: Education here prepared students for the workforce, as did education at the Boston Trade School, Wentworth, and Northeastern. In this photograph, a class of female nurses being educated by the Church school pose for a photograph sometime in the 1940s.
caption.title: Student nurses at Mission Church School

caption.text: These centers for adult and continuing education shared streets with public education for children. Here, adolescents read in one of the branches of the Boston Public Library. The room is not dissimilar from the classroom at the Boston Industrial School, with low bookshelves, framed paintings on the walls, and hanging lights.
caption.title: Parker Street Branch of the BPL

caption.text: At the library too, however, public education was not just aimed at children. This article, from 1928 promotes adult education classes the BPL offered in partnership with universities and museums. The article states that these courses are meant to help attendees advance in their professions and trades, but also to make “education as a way of living” accessible to all.
caption.title: Adult education at the BPL

caption.text: Down the street from the Parker branch of the BPL, and across Huntington Avenue is Harvard Medical School, which first appears on the Bromley Atlas in this space in 1906…
caption.title: Harvard Medical School

caption.text: The medical school buildings four years later, in 1910…
By 1927, when this photograph was taken, the neighborhood was firmly established as a center for medical research and hospital treatment…
caption.title: Boston Lying-In Hospital

caption.text: Like the vocational schools and library, Harvard Medical College also offered public education. This lecture, advertised in 1928, focused specifically on education in health and safety, emphasizing the modern risks arising from the use of gas and electricity.
caption.title: Public Lecture at Harvard Medical College

caption.text: The Boston Globe, the newspaper that reported on the lecture itself also participated in public education, but summarizing the contents of the lecture and publishing them the following day
caption.title: Globe account of the lecture

caption.text: Later that same year the *Boston Globe* summarized another lecture given by Harvard, this one more generally educational, rather than practical. Other muscles, according to the article, could contract either weakly or powerfully, while the heart could contract powerfully or not at all.
caption.title: Lecture on the heart

caption.text: The busy Huntington Avenue separated Harvard Medical School and other nearby hospitals from the streets where Mission Main would be built. It is likely the street, between the two neighborhoods, that those attending lectures would have taken to reach the lecture hall.
caption.title: Huntington Avenue

caption.text: While large institutions like Harvard Medical School, the Boston Public Library, and Wentworth Institute were not displaced by the construction of Mission Main, the single family lots visible here would have been torn down to make way for the Housing Project.
caption.title: Housing project lots

caption.text: This aerial view of Mission Main shows the project around 1955, the row of a dozen apartment complexes on the blocks between St Alphonsus Street and Parker Street.
caption.title: Aerial view, with the housing project center right

caption.text: In 2002, the Boston Housing Authority completed the re-construction of Mission Main. Today, the buildings are lower, three stories, like the Mission Extension, and the street running past the houses has a divider of grass and small trees. This picture is from April of 2026.
caption.title: Mission Main site today
